name: org.machanism.machai:mcp-server-maven-plugin
description: MCP Server Maven Plugin is a Java 17 Maven plugin that starts the Machai MCP Server as part of a Maven build and exposes Machai AI tools over the Model Context Protocol via HTTP. It is intended for Maven-driven local development, integration testing, demonstrations, and workflows that require a running MCP endpoint alongside a build without a separate launcher. The plugin bridges Maven project context with the Machai MCP Server runtime by resolving the project base directory, project name, and version from the active Maven project, constructing the appropriate server instance, registering available tools through Java SPI discovery, and starting the server on the configured port. It provides two aggregator goals: mcp-server:stateless for independent stateless HTTP requests using the MCP SDK stateless servlet transport, and mcp-server:streamable for incremental result delivery over persistent HTTP connections using streamable HTTP transport. The org.machanism.machai.mcp.maven package provides abstract and concrete Mojos plus shared configuration logic for environment variables and credentials, while org.machanism.machai.mcp.maven.tools provides lifecycle utilities such as stopping the server.

usageContext: Starting a Machai MCP Server directly from a Maven goal
Exposing SPI-discovered Machai AI tools to MCP-compatible clients over HTTP
Running stateless HTTP MCP endpoints where each request is handled independently
Running streamable HTTP MCP endpoints for incremental result delivery over persistent HTTP connections
Using Maven project metadata such as project name, version, and base directory as server identity and runtime context
Providing a temporary MCP endpoint during development, integration testing, demonstrations, or build-adjacent automation
Configuring the server port through the mcp.port Maven parameter or command-line property

constructors.description: Creates the Maven Mojo for the mcp-server:stateless aggregator goal. Maven instantiates this component when the stateless goal is invoked, injects the active Maven project, base directory, and mcp.port configuration, and then starts a stateless HTTP MCP server.

examples.description: Install and configure the plugin in pom.xml. Maven creates org.machanism.machai.mcp.maven.HttpStatelessServerMojo automatically when the stateless goal is invoked, so application code normally does not instantiate it directly.

constructors.description: Creates the Maven Mojo for the mcp-server:streamable aggregator goal. Maven instantiates this component when the streamable goal is invoked, injects the active project context and configured port, and starts a streamable HTTP MCP server for persistent HTTP transport workflows.

examples.description: Install and configure the plugin in pom.xml. Maven creates org.machanism.machai.mcp.maven.HttpStreamableMcpServerMojo automatically when the streamable goal is invoked.

constructors.description: Creates a lifecycle utility object that provides utility functions for managing the MCP server lifecycle from plugin-related tool integrations, including clean server shutdown.

examples.description: Create the lifecycle tool directly in Java for tests or embedded experiments that need to exercise the MCP server management functions outside normal Maven goal execution.

features.name: mcp-server:stateless
features.description: Starts the Machai MCP Server in stateless HTTP mode as an aggregator Maven goal. The goal constructs the stateless server variant, registers tools through Java SPI discovery, reports server identity from the Maven project name and version, uses the Maven project base directory as the project directory, and listens on the configured mcp.port until the process is stopped.

examples.description: How to use it: install or reference the plugin, provide the required mcp.port value, and run the stateless goal from the project directory to expose an MCP-compatible HTTP endpoint for local development or testing.
Invoke the fully qualified Maven plugin goal when the plugin prefix is not configured or when you want an explicit versioned command.

features.name: mcp-server:streamable
features.description: Starts the Machai MCP Server in streamable HTTP mode as an aggregator Maven goal. The goal constructs the streamable server variant, registers the same SPI-discovered Machai tools as the stateless mode, reports Maven project name and version as server identity, uses the Maven base directory as runtime project context, and listens on the configured mcp.port until stopped.

examples.description: How to use it: configure or reference the plugin, provide mcp.port, and run the streamable goal when MCP client workflows benefit from incremental result delivery over a persistent HTTP connection.
Invoke the streamable goal with fully qualified Maven coordinates for reproducible command-line usage.

features.name: stopMcpServer
features.description: Provides a lifecycle utility function for stopping the MCP server. It is intended to let tool integrations or clients request clean termination of the server after build-adjacent MCP interactions are complete.

examples.description: Call the utility directly in Java to request a normal shutdown using exit code 0 during tests or embedded experiments.
How to use it from an MCP-style client: after connecting to the Maven-started server and completing work, invoke the published shutdown tool with exit_code 0 to terminate the server cleanly.

customizations.name: mcp.port
customizations.description: Required Maven plugin configuration parameter that defines the network port used by both the stateless and streamable HTTP MCP server goals. It can be supplied in the plugin configuration block or overridden on the command line with -Dmcp.port=....

examples.description: Configure the HTTP port in pom.xml so developers can run mcp-server:stateless or mcp-server:streamable without repeating the port value on every command.
Override or provide the required port from the command line for a one-off run.

customizations.name: basedir
customizations.description: Automatically resolved Maven project base directory. The plugin passes this directory to the server runtime so SPI-loaded tools can resolve project-relative paths during MCP interactions.

examples.description: Run the goal from the Maven project. The plugin resolves the current project base directory automatically and uses it as the MCP server project directory.

customizations.name: project name and version
customizations.description: Maven project metadata resolved from the active project and reported in the MCP server identity block exposed to connecting clients.

examples.description: The active Maven project name and version are used by the plugin when identifying the running MCP server to clients.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.mcp.maven.AbstractMCPServerMojo
studs.description: Abstract base class for MCP server Maven plugin Mojos. It declares and centralizes the shared Maven configuration context used by concrete goal classes, including basedir, project, and mcp.port, and is extended by the stateless and streamable server Mojo implementations.

examples.description: Example of extending the shared abstract base class when creating a custom Maven Mojo that needs the same MCP server configuration context.

examples.title: Install and run the stateless MCP server goal
examples.scenario: Start a stateless MCP-compatible HTTP endpoint from a Maven project for local development, integration testing, or demonstrations.
steps: Add org.machanism.machai:mcp-server-maven-plugin:1.2.0-SNAPSHOT to the build/plugins section of pom.xml or invoke it with fully qualified Maven coordinates.
Configure the required port in the plugin configuration using <port>8080</port>, or plan to pass it from the command line as -Dmcp.port=8080.
Run mvn mcp-server:stateless -Dmcp.port=8080 from the Maven project directory.
Connect an MCP-compatible client to the HTTP endpoint opened by the plugin-started server.
Use the exposed Machai tools during the development or test session, then stop the Maven process or invoke the shutdown tool.
examples.description: Practical installation, configuration, and execution flow for the ready-to-use stateless Maven goal. Stateless mode handles each HTTP request independently and is suitable for simple local MCP endpoint scenarios.

examples.title: Install and run the streamable MCP server goal
examples.scenario: Start a streamable HTTP MCP endpoint when a client workflow benefits from incremental result delivery over a persistent HTTP connection.
steps: Declare the plugin org.machanism.machai:mcp-server-maven-plugin:1.2.0-SNAPSHOT in pom.xml or call it using fully qualified coordinates.
Set the required HTTP port with <port>8081</port> in the plugin configuration or pass -Dmcp.port=8081 on the command line.
Run mvn mcp-server:streamable -Dmcp.port=8081 from the active Maven project.
Connect your MCP-compatible client to the streamable HTTP endpoint and use the same SPI-discovered Machai tools available in stateless mode.
When finished, request clean shutdown through the lifecycle tool or stop the Maven process.
examples.description: Practical installation, configuration, and execution flow for the streamable Maven goal. Streamable mode supports incremental result delivery through streamable HTTP transport while still using Maven project context for server identity and project directory resolution.

examples.title: Stop the Maven-started MCP server with the lifecycle tool
examples.scenario: Gracefully terminate the MCP server after completing client interactions.
steps: Start the MCP server with either mvn mcp-server:stateless -Dmcp.port=8080 or mvn mcp-server:streamable -Dmcp.port=8081.
Perform the required interactions from an MCP-compatible client.
Invoke the server lifecycle tool named stop_mcp_server with exit_code set to 0 for a normal shutdown.
Wait for the Maven-hosted server process to terminate cleanly.
examples.description: Practical how-to scenario for using the lifecycle utility supplied by org.machanism.machai.mcp.maven.tools.MCPServerTools to stop a plugin-started server from a client-oriented workflow.
